blocks.text: supervised the holy work with very great care, assisting the elders of the Jews and the chief officers of the temple.
The holy work prospered, while the prophets Haggai and Zechariah prophesied;
and they completed it by the command of the Lord God of Israel. So with the consent of Cyrus and Darius and Artaxerxes, kings of the Persians,

4 : The reference to I (465–424 ; see 8.1–9.36) is taken from Ezra 6.14, where it functions ideologically, linking all the stages of the return. The verse is more problematic in 1 Esd because, unlike Ezra-Nehemiah (see Neh 1–6), this book does not depict any Temple-building activities under Artaxerxes; Josephus omits the name.
the holy house was finished by the twenty-third day of the month of Adar, in the sixth year of King Darius.
And the people of Israel, the priests, the Levites, and the rest of those who returned from exile who joined them, did according to what was written in the book of Moses.
They offered at the dedication of the temple of the Lord one hundred bulls, two hundred rams, four hundred lambs,
and twelve male goats for the sin of all Israel, according to the number of the twelve leaders of the tribes of Israel;
7–8 : Compare the account of the dedication of Solomon's Temple (1 Kings 8.8,63).

and the priests and the Levites stood arrayed in their vestments, according to kindred, for the services of the Lord God of Israel in accordance with the book of Moses; and the gatekeepers were at each gate.
7.1–9: The completion of the Temple (Ezra 6.13–18). At last, the builders are able to complete work on the Temple in 515 and celebrate their success.
9 : Compare Ezra 6.18.
The people of Israel who came from exile kept the passover on the fourteenth day of the first month, after the priests and the Levites were purified together.
Not all of the returned captives were purified, but the Levites were all purified together,
11 : , this comment is not in Ezra 6, but is perhaps borrowed from 2 Chr 30.18.
and they sacrificed the passover lamb for all the returned captives and for their kindred the priests and for themselves.
The people of Israel who had returned from exile ate it, all those who had separated themselves from the abominations of the peoples of the land and sought the Lord.
13 : Contrary to Ezra 6.21, the account here suggests that only the returned exiles participated in the Passover, but see 5.50n.
They also kept the festival of unleavened bread seven days, rejoicing before the Lord,
because he had changed the will of the king of the Assyrians concerning them, to strengthen their hands for the service of the Lord God of Israel.

2.1–9.55: Stages of return and restoration. Except for the new material in 3.1–5.6, from here on 1 Esd follows Ezra 1–10 and Neh 8, reshaping the material through artful composition that shifts the emphases from the community to exceptional leaders, mostly Zerubbabel the governor, and Ezra the priest and scribe. Some changes in sequence highlight these emphases (see 2.16–30).

3.1–7.15: Zerubbabel leads the return and the rebuilding of the altar and the Temple. In 1 Esd, Samarian opponents successfully stop rebuilding before any restoration takes place (in contrast to Ezra 3–6, where Zerubbabel and Jeshua lead the laying of the Temple's foundation before being stopped). This arrangement of the narrative magnifies the accomplishments of Zerubbabel, making him responsible for the very first successful return. Here, then, Zerubbabel wins King Darius's favor and as a result receives permission to go and restore the Temple.
7.10–15: The Passover celebration (Ezra 6.19–22). The celebration marks the restoration of Jewish life, resuming life and practices that were first disrupted with Josiah's death in ch 1.
15 : , an anachronistic reference to the Persian king, alluding to the earliest exile by Assyria, which is now reversed. Josephus ( 11.4.8) refers to the Persian king.
